URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Typhoon_Haiyan

SUMMARY:
The 30th named storm, thirteenth typhoon, and fifth super typhoon of the 2013 Pacific typhoon season, Haiyan originated from an area of low pressure several hundred kilometers east-southeast of Pohnpei in the Federated States of Micronesia on November 2. Shortly before Haiyan entered the Philippine Area of Responsibility on November 6, PAGASA raised Public Storm Warning Signal (PSWS) No.1, the lowest of four levels, for much of the Visayas and Mindanao. Typhoon Haiyan, called Yolanda in the Philippines, caused catastrophic damage throughout much of the islands of Leyte "Leyte (province)"), where cities and towns were largely destroyed.

**Typhoon Haiyan**, known in the Philippines as **Super Typhoon Yolanda**, was one of [the most powerful](/wiki/List_of_the_most_intense_tropical_cyclones "List of the most intense tropical cyclones") [tropical cyclones](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone "Tropical cyclone") ever recorded. Upon making landfall, Haiyan devastated portions of Southeast Asia, particularly the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines") during early November 2013.[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is one of [the deadliest typhoons on record in the Philippines](/wiki/Typhoons_in_the_Philippines "Typhoons in the Philippines"),[[2]](#cite_note-2) killing at least 6,300 people in the region of Visayas alone.[[3]](#cite_note-NDRRMC-3) In terms of JTWC-estimated 1-minute sustained winds, Haiyan is tied with [Meranti](/wiki/Typhoon_Meranti "Typhoon Meranti") in [2016](/wiki/2016_Pacific_typhoon_season "2016 Pacific typhoon season") for being the second strongest [landfalling](/wiki/Landfall_(meteorology) "Landfall (meteorology)") tropical cyclone on record, only behind [Goni](/wiki/Typhoon_Goni "Typhoon Goni") of [2020](/wiki/2020_Pacific_typhoon_season "2020 Pacific typhoon season"). It was also the most intense tropical cyclone [worldwide in 2013](/wiki/Tropical_cyclones_in_2013 "Tropical cyclones in 2013").

The 30th [named storm](/wiki/Named_storm "Named storm"), thirteenth typhoon, and fifth [super typhoon](/wiki/Super_typhoon "Super typhoon") of the [2013 Pacific typhoon season](/wiki/2013_Pacific_typhoon_season "2013 Pacific typhoon season"), Haiyan originated from an [area of low pressure](/wiki/Area_of_low_pressure "Area of low pressure") several hundred kilometers east-southeast of [Pohnpei](/wiki/Pohnpei "Pohnpei") in the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia") on November 2. Tracking generally westward, environmental conditions favored [tropical cyclogenesis](/wiki/Tropical_cyclogenesis "Tropical cyclogenesis") and the system developed into a tropical depression on the following day. After becoming a tropical storm and being named *Haiyan* at 00:00 [UTC](/wiki/UTC "UTC") on November 4, the system began a period of [rapid intensification](/wiki/Rapid_intensification "Rapid intensification") that brought it to [typhoon](/wiki/Typhoon "Typhoon") intensity by 18:00 UTC on November 5. By November 6, the [Joint Typhoon Warning Center](/wiki/Joint_Typhoon_Warning_Center "Joint Typhoon Warning Center") (JTWC) assessed the system as a Category 5-equivalent super typhoon on the [Saffir–Simpson hurricane wind scale](/wiki/Saffir%E2%80%93Simpson_scale "Saffir–Simpson scale") (SSHWS); the storm passed over the island of [Kayangel](/wiki/Kayangel "Kayangel") in Palau shortly after attaining this strength.

The [Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration](/wiki/Philippine_Atmospheric,_Geophysical_and_Astronomical_Services_Administration "Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration") (PAGASA) estimated the average ten-minute sustained winds at 235 km/h (146 mph) and gusts up to 275 km/h (171 mph) at landfall over [Guiuan, Eastern Samar](/wiki/Guiuan,_Eastern_Samar "Guiuan, Eastern Samar"). Haiyan continued to intensify; at 12:00 UTC on November 7, the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") (JMA) upgraded the storm's maximum ten-minute sustained winds to a peak of 230 km/h (140 mph). The [Hong Kong Observatory](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Observatory "Hong Kong Observatory") put the storm's maximum ten-minute sustained winds at 285 km/h (175 mph)[[4]](#cite_note-4) prior to landfall in the central Philippines, while the [China Meteorological Administration](/wiki/China_Meteorological_Administration "China Meteorological Administration") (CMA) estimated the maximum two-minute sustained winds at the time to be around 78 m/s or 280 km/h (170 mph). At the same time, the JTWC estimated the system's one-minute sustained winds at 315 km/h (195 mph), unofficially making Haiyan the strongest tropical cyclone ever observed based on wind speed, a record which would later be surpassed by [Hurricane Patricia](/wiki/Hurricane_Patricia "Hurricane Patricia") in 2015 at 345 km/h (215 mph).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Haiyan is also tied with [Meranti](/wiki/Typhoon_Meranti "Typhoon Meranti") in 2016, [Goni](/wiki/Typhoon_Goni "Typhoon Goni") in 2020 and [Surigae](/wiki/Typhoon_Surigae "Typhoon Surigae") in 2021 as the most intense tropical cyclone in the [Eastern Hemisphere](/wiki/Eastern_Hemisphere "Eastern Hemisphere") by 1-minute sustained winds; several others have recorded lower central pressure readings. At 20:40 [UTC](/wiki/Coordinated_Universal_Time "Coordinated Universal Time") on November 7, the eye of the typhoon made its first [landfall](/wiki/Landfall "Landfall") in the Philippines at [Guiuan, Eastern Samar](/wiki/Guiuan,_Eastern_Samar "Guiuan, Eastern Samar") at peak strength. Gradually weakening, the storm made five additional landfalls in the country before emerging over the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea"). Turning northwestward, the typhoon eventually struck northern [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam "Vietnam") as a severe tropical storm on November 10. Haiyan was last noted as a tropical depression by the JMA on the following day.

The first warning noted for Haiyan was in November 3, when a storm warning arose in the Federated States of Micronesia, specifically in the [Chuuk Lagoon](/wiki/Chuuk_Lagoon "Chuuk Lagoon"), [Losap](/wiki/Losap "Losap"), and [Poluwat](/wiki/Poluwat "Poluwat"), gradually expanding to other towns as well. Warnings rose for a second time in Micronesia, before being discontinued. In the Philippines, PAGASA raised Signal No. 1 on November 6, before the landfall of Haiyan. More provinces were included, until Signal No. 4, the highest warning, was raised. Other preparations were made, such as class suspensions and evacuations. In China, an emergency was declared in three provinces, causing vessels to be brought back to shore. In Vietnam, the highest emergency level was announced, causing thousands of people to be evacuated.

In Micronesia, heavy rains scattered in most of the places, causing one canoe house and three other houses to be destroyed. Other than houses, many trees were downed. In Palau, houses were also destroyed. Power outages were reported, with a total of 69 people being displaced. In Taiwan, eight people died due to strong waves. One person was also declared missing in Hong Kong. In Southern China, extensive flooding occurred, killing 30 people and destroying 900 homes. In Vietnam, heavy rains battered the country, killing 18 people and injuring 93.

The typhoon caused catastrophic destruction in the [Visayas](/wiki/Visayas "Visayas"), particularly in the islands of [Samar](/wiki/Samar_island "Samar island") and [Leyte](/wiki/Leyte_island "Leyte island"). According to UN officials, about 11 million people were affected and many were left homeless; many people are still missing as a result of this storm.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

Due to its extensive deaths and damages, the name Haiyan was retired in 2014 and replaced with [Bailu](/wiki/Typhoon_Bailu "Typhoon Bailu"). It was first used in the [2019 season](/wiki/2019_Pacific_typhoon_season "2019 Pacific typhoon season").

## Meteorological history

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2c/Haiyan_2013_path.png/330px-Haiyan_2013_path.png)
![circle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/27/Disc_Plain_black.svg/20px-Disc_Plain_black.svg.png)
![square](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/21/Solid_black.svg/20px-Solid_black.svg.png)
![triangle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/ArrowUp.svg/20px-ArrowUp.svg.png)

On November 2, the [Joint Typhoon Warning Center](/wiki/Joint_Typhoon_Warning_Center "Joint Typhoon Warning Center") (JTWC) began monitoring a broad [low-pressure area](/wiki/Low-pressure_area_(meteorology) "Low-pressure area (meteorology)") about 425 kilometers (264 miles) east-southeast of [Pohnpei](/wiki/Pohnpei "Pohnpei"), one of the states in the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia").[[nb 1]](#cite_note-8) As the system moved through a region favoring [tropical cyclogenesis](/wiki/Tropical_cyclogenesis "Tropical cyclogenesis"),[[8]](#cite_note-9) the [Japan Meteorological Agency](/wiki/Japan_Meteorological_Agency "Japan Meteorological Agency") (JMA) classified it as a [tropical depression](/wiki/Tropical_depression "Tropical depression") early on November 3.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10)[[nb 2]](#cite_note-12)

The system quickly intensified into a [tropical storm](/wiki/Tropical_storm "Tropical storm"), prompting the JMA to [assign it the name](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone_naming "Tropical cyclone naming") *Haiyan* ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 海燕; lit. '[petrel](/wiki/Petrel "Petrel")') at 00:00 UTC on November 4.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10) Tracking generally westward along the southern periphery of a [subtropical ridge](/wiki/Subtropical_ridge "Subtropical ridge"),[[11]](#cite_note-13) [rapid intensification](/wiki/Rapid_intensification "Rapid intensification") ensued by November 5 as a [central dense overcast](/wiki/Central_dense_overcast "Central dense overcast") with an embedded [eye](/wiki/Eye_(cyclone) "Eye (cyclone)") developing; the JMA classified Haiyan as a typhoon later that day.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10) On November 6, the [Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration](/wiki/Philippine_Atmospheric,_Geophysical_and_Astronomical_Services_Administration "Philippine Atmospheric, Geophysical and Astronomical Services Administration") (PAGASA) assigned the storm the local name *Yolanda* as it approached the [Philippine Area of Responsibility](/wiki/Philippine_Area_of_Responsibility "Philippine Area of Responsibility").[[12]](#cite_note-14)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Comparison_between_Haiyan_and_Katrina.png/250px-Comparison_between_Haiyan_and_Katrina.png)

Intensification slowed somewhat during the day, though the JTWC estimated the storm to have attained [Category 5-equivalent](/wiki/Category_5_hurricane "Category 5 hurricane") super typhoon status on the [Saffir–Simpson hurricane wind scale](/wiki/Saffir%E2%80%93Simpson_scale "Saffir–Simpson scale") (SSHWS) around 12:00 UTC.[[nb 3]](#cite_note-STY-16)[[14]](#cite_note-17) Later, the eye of the typhoon passed over the island of [Kayangel](/wiki/Kayangel "Kayangel") in Palau.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18)

Around 12:00 UTC on November 7, Haiyan attained ten-minute sustained winds of 230 km/h (140 mph) and a minimum [central pressure](/wiki/Barometric_pressure "Barometric pressure") of 895 [mbar](/wiki/Mbar "Mbar") (hPa; 26.43 [inHg](/wiki/Inch_of_mercury "Inch of mercury")).[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10) Six hours later, the JTWC estimated Haiyan to have attained one-minute sustained winds of 315 km/h (196 mph) and gusts up to 380 km/h (240 mph).[[16]](#cite_note-JTWC-PR19-19) The storm displayed some characteristics of an [annular tropical cyclone](/wiki/Annular_tropical_cyclone "Annular tropical cyclone"), though a strong convective band remained present along the western side of the system.[[16]](#cite_note-JTWC-PR19-19)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Haiyan_2013_landfall.gif/250px-Haiyan_2013_landfall.gif)

At 20:40 UTC on November 7, Haiyan made [landfall](/wiki/Landfall_(meteorology) "Landfall (meteorology)") in [Guiuan](/wiki/Guiuan "Guiuan"), [Eastern Samar](/wiki/Eastern_Samar "Eastern Samar") at peak intensity.[[17]](#cite_note-webcitation1-20) The JTWC's unofficial estimate of one-minute sustained winds of 305 km/h (190 mph) would, by that measure, make Haiyan the most powerful storm ever recorded to strike land. This record was later broken by [Typhoon Goni](/wiki/Typhoon_Goni "Typhoon Goni") in 2020.[[18]](#cite_note-WU-21)[[19]](#cite_note-JeffMNov7-22) Interaction with land caused a slight degradation of the storm's structure, though it remained an exceptionally-powerful storm when it struck [Tolosa, Leyte](/wiki/Tolosa,_Leyte "Tolosa, Leyte") around 23:00 UTC.[[20]](#cite_note-23) The typhoon made four additional landfalls as it traversed the Visayas:[[21]](#cite_note-24) [Daanbantayan](/wiki/Daanbantayan,_Cebu "Daanbantayan, Cebu") and [Bantayan Island](/wiki/Bantayan_Island "Bantayan Island") in [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu_(province) "Cebu (province)"), [Concepcion](/wiki/Concepcion,_Iloilo "Concepcion, Iloilo") in [Iloilo](/wiki/Iloilo_(province) "Iloilo (province)"), and [Busuanga Island](/wiki/Busuanga_Island "Busuanga Island") in [Palawan](/wiki/Palawan "Palawan").

Haiyan, with its core disrupted by landfall interaction with the Philippines, emerged over the [South China Sea](/wiki/South_China_Sea "South China Sea") late on November 8.[[22]](#cite_note-25) Environmental conditions ahead of the storm soon became less favorable, as cool stable air began wrapping into the western side of the storm's circulation.[[23]](#cite_note-26) Continuing across the South China Sea, Haiyan turned more northwesterly late on November 9 and through November 10, as it moved around the southwestern edge of the subtropical ridge previously steering it westward.[[24]](#cite_note-27) Rapid weakening ensued as Haiyan approached its final landfall in Vietnam,[[25]](#cite_note-28) ultimately moving ashore in the country near [Haiphong](/wiki/Haiphong "Haiphong") around 21:00 UTC, as a severe tropical storm.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10) Once onshore, the storm quickly deteriorated and was last noted as it dissipated over [Guangxi Zhuang Autonomous Region](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"), China, on November 11.[[9]](#cite_note-Haiyan_BT-10)

## Preparations

### Micronesia and Palau

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3e/Haiyan_2013-11-06_0225Z.png/250px-Haiyan_2013-11-06_0225Z.png)

Upon JTWC's declaration of Tropical Depression 31W on November 3, a [tropical storm warning](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone_warnings_and_watches "Tropical cyclone warnings and watches") was issued for [Chuuk Lagoon](/wiki/Chuuk_Lagoon "Chuuk Lagoon"), [Losap](/wiki/Losap "Losap"), and [Poluwat](/wiki/Poluwat "Poluwat") in the [Federated States of Micronesia](/wiki/Federated_States_of_Micronesia "Federated States of Micronesia"). Further west, [Faraulep](/wiki/Faraulep "Faraulep"), [Satawal](/wiki/Satawal "Satawal"), and [Woleai](/wiki/Woleai "Woleai"), were placed under a [typhoon watch](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone_warnings_and_watches "Tropical cyclone warnings and watches") while [Fananu](/wiki/Fananu "Fananu") and [Ulul](/wiki/Ulul "Ulul") were placed under a [tropical storm watch](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone_warnings_and_watches "Tropical cyclone warnings and watches").[[26]](#cite_note-29) The following day, the tropical storm warning expanded to include Satawal while a [typhoon warning](/wiki/Tropical_cyclone_warnings_and_watches "Tropical cyclone warnings and watches") was issued for Woleai.[[27]](#cite_note-30) Much of [Yap State](/wiki/Yap_State "Yap State") and the islands of [Koror](/wiki/Koror "Koror") and [Kayangel](/wiki/Kayangel "Kayangel") in Palau were placed under a typhoon watch.[[28]](#cite_note-haiyan_advisory_6-31) The government issued a mandatory evacuation for Kayangel, and although most residents ignored the warning, they all survived the storm.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18) As Haiyan progressed westward, the easternmost advisories were gradually discontinued.[[28]](#cite_note-haiyan_advisory_6-31) As Haiyan intensified into a typhoon on November 5, warnings were raised across Palau and Yap State.[[29]](#cite_note-32)[[30]](#cite_note-33) Government offices in [Melekeok](/wiki/Melekeok "Melekeok") were used as an evacuation building for Palau.[[31]](#cite_note-ocha117-34) Despite mandatory evacuation orders, most residents on Kayangel remained on the island and rode out the typhoon.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18)

### Philippines

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/YolandaRainfallWarning.png/250px-YolandaRainfallWarning.png)

Shortly before Haiyan entered the Philippine Area of Responsibility on November 6, PAGASA raised [Public Storm Warning Signal](/wiki/Typhoons_in_the_Philippines#Public_Storm_Warning_Signals "Typhoons in the Philippines") (PSWS) No.1, the lowest of four levels, for much of the [Visayas](/wiki/Visayas "Visayas") and [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao").[[32]](#cite_note-35) As the storm continued to approach the country, warnings expanded into [Luzon](/wiki/Luzon "Luzon") and increased in severity for eastern areas.[[33]](#cite_note-36) By the evening of November 7, PSWS No. 4, the highest level of warning which indicates winds in excess of 185 km/h (115 mph) are expected, was raised for [Biliran Island](/wiki/Biliran_Island "Biliran Island"), [Eastern Samar](/wiki/Eastern_Samar "Eastern Samar"), [Leyte](/wiki/Leyte_(province) "Leyte (province)"), Northern [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu "Cebu"), [Metro Cebu](/wiki/Metro_Cebu "Metro Cebu"), [Samar](/wiki/Samar "Samar"), and [Southern Leyte](/wiki/Southern_Leyte "Southern Leyte").[[34]](#cite_note-37)[[35]](#cite_note-38) Through November 8, the coverage of PSWS No. 4 continued to expand, with areas in southern Luzon being included.[[36]](#cite_note-39)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Yolanda_PSWS_Map.png/250px-Yolanda_PSWS_Map.png)

Officials placed police officers in the [Bicol Region](/wiki/Bicol_Region "Bicol Region") ahead of the storm.[[37]](#cite_note-40) In the provinces of [Samar](/wiki/Samar "Samar") and [Leyte](/wiki/Leyte "Leyte"), classes were canceled, and residents in flood- and landslide-prone areas were required to evacuate.[[38]](#cite_note-41) Some of the storm-threatened areas were affected by an earlier [earthquake in Bohol](/wiki/2013_Bohol_earthquake "2013 Bohol earthquake").[[39]](#cite_note-42) Then-Philippine President [Noynoy Aquino](/wiki/Noynoy_Aquino "Noynoy Aquino") requested the military to deploy planes and helicopters to the region expected to be affected.[[40]](#cite_note-43) As Haiyan was moving very fast, [PAGASA](/wiki/PAGASA "PAGASA") issued warnings at different levels to about 60 of the 80 provinces, including the capital [Metro Manila](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila").[[41]](#cite_note-44) On November 8, the [International Charter on Space and Major Disasters](/wiki/International_Charter_on_Space_and_Major_Disasters "International Charter on Space and Major Disasters") was activated, providing widespread charitable satellite coverage to relief organizations.[[42]](#cite_note-45)

### Southern China

The State Flood Control and Drought Relief Headquarters hoisted a level three emergency response in the provinces of [Hainan](/wiki/Hainan "Hainan"), Guangdong and [Guangxi](/wiki/Guangxi "Guangxi"). All fishing vessels were urged to return to ports by noon on November 9.[[43]](#cite_note-english.peopledaily.com.cn-46) The [Hong Kong Observatory](/wiki/Hong_Kong_Observatory "Hong Kong Observatory") issued the Strong Monsoon Signal at 19:10 HKT on November 9,[[44]](#cite_note-47) and it was still in place on November 13.[[45]](#cite_note-48)

### Vietnam

On November 8, Prime Minister [Nguyễn Tấn Dũng](/wiki/Nguy%E1%BB%85n_T%E1%BA%A5n_D%C5%A9ng "Nguyễn Tấn Dũng") activated the highest state of preparedness in the country.[[46]](#cite_note-VietnameRW1-49) Approximately 600,000 people across southern and central provinces were evacuated while a further 200,000 were evacuated in northern provinces. Alerts were sent to 85,328 seagoing vessels, with a collective crew of 385,372 people, to sail to safer waters away from the storm. Requests were sent to China, Malaysia, Indonesia, and the Philippines to aid any fishermen who needed immediate shelter from the typhoon.[[47]](#cite_note-VietnamRW2-50) Threatening Vietnam after two other typhoons, [Wutip](/wiki/Typhoon_Wutip_(2013) "Typhoon Wutip (2013)") and [Nari](/wiki/Typhoon_Nari_(2013) "Typhoon Nari (2013)"), there were concerns that the storm would cause significant damage to homes with makeshift repairs.[[48]](#cite_note-51) Roughly 460,000 military personnel and other authorities were mobilized to assist in evacuation efforts.[[47]](#cite_note-VietnamRW2-50) Hundreds of flights were canceled across the country while schools were closed on November 11. On the small island of [Cồn Cỏ](/wiki/C%E1%BB%93n_C%E1%BB%8F_District "Cồn Cỏ District"), all residents were moved to underground shelters with enough supplies for several days.[[49]](#cite_note-BBCNov10-52) The [International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies](/wiki/International_Federation_of_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Societies "International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies") (IFRC) branches in Vietnam prepared relief stockpiles, consisting of food, water, housing material, and [₫](/wiki/Vietnamese_%C4%91%E1%BB%93ng "Vietnamese đồng")6.6 billion (US$310,000) in funds.[[47]](#cite_note-VietnamRW2-50) The local United Nations Resident Coordinator, Pratibha Mehta, praised the government's actions and credited them with saving numerous lives.[[46]](#cite_note-VietnameRW1-49) However, there were complaints from many residents that the warnings came too late.[[49]](#cite_note-BBCNov10-52)

### Micronesia

As the storm brushed Eauripik, strong winds and heavy rain battered much of Micronesia. In Eauripik, one canoe house and three residential properties were damaged and banana and breadfruit trees were damaged. In Woleai, banana and breadfruit trees were damaged. In Ifalik, minor inundation at coastal areas and banana and breadfruit trees were damaged.[[51]](#cite_note-54)

### Palau

On Kayangel in Palau, a high [storm surge](/wiki/Storm_surge "Storm surge") damaged several houses,[[31]](#cite_note-ocha117-34) while strong winds downed trees.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18) Despite residents' refusal to evacuate, no fatalities or major injuries took place on the island. Helicopters were flown to the island to survey the damage and provide relief supplies. The government planned to evacuate those who were left homeless from the island.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18) Koror, [Babeldaob](/wiki/Babeldaob "Babeldaob") and Kayangel each lost access to water and power.[[15]](#cite_note-ABC1-18) In Koror, winds reaching as high as 120 km/h (75 mph) blew out rooftops and downed trees and power lines. A causeway linking an offshore hospital to the main island was temporarily shut down after being inundated by water.[[52]](#cite_note-OCHAFlashUpdate3-55) On the northern end of Babeldaob, Haiyan damaged schools and buildings.[[31]](#cite_note-ocha117-34) Lying closest to Haiyan at the time of the typhoon's passage, Kayangel was flooded in its entirety, and all homes were destroyed. Though no people were killed there, 69 others were displaced by the storm.[[52]](#cite_note-OCHAFlashUpdate3-55)

# Typhoon Haiyan: Facts, FAQs, and how to help

![A girl stands amid coconut trees downed by Typhoon Haiyan.](https://wvusstatic.com/www/uploads/2018/06/D310-0736-1181_435050-850x567.jpg)

Baby Claire (pictured at 9), who was a sponsored child, stands near her home, which was smashed by falling coconut palm trees during Typhoon Haiyan in 2013. Her family survived by running to a nearby community center. (©2013 World Vision/photo by Jon Warren)

Baby Claire (pictured at 9), who was a sponsored child, stands near her home, which was smashed by falling coconut palm trees during Typhoon Haiyan in 2013. Her family survived by running to a nearby community center. (©2013 World Vision/photo by Jon Warren)

Super typhoon Haiyan, one of the most powerful typhoons in history, struck the Philippines on November 8, 2013, as a Category 5 storm. The typhoon’s fury affected more than 14 million people across 44 provinces, claiming the lives of over 8,000 people, and displacing millions more. Typhoon Haiyan’s aftermath underscored the importance of disaster preparedness, response coordination, and ongoing support to foster resilience in the face of nature’s impact.

After the storm, the Philippines embarked on a path of recovery and rebuilding. With hope for the future, determination, and resilience, the people of the Philippines gradually restored the impacted areas.

## Typhoon Haiyan: Facts, FAQs, and how to help

Explore Typhoon Haiyan facts and frequently asked questions, and learn how you can help.

## Fast facts: Typhoon Haiyan

[BACK TO QUESTIONS](#FAQs)

![A young girl looks into the camera, with family and other typhoon survivors surrounded by relief supplies.](https://wvusstatic.com/email/met3/spacer.gif)

## Where did Typhoon Haiyan hit?

Typhoon Haiyan first struck Palau, an island in the western Pacific, and Micronesia. Haiyan then made landfall in the Philippines as a [Category 5 storm](https://www.nhc.noaa.gov/aboutsshws.php) with wind speeds above 157 mph. After hitting the Philippines, the storm diminished as it moved into the South China Sea toward Vietnam, finally disintegrating into rain over Guanxi, China.

[BACK TO QUESTIONS](#FAQs)

## Is the Philippines a disaster-prone country?

The Philippines ranks among the world’s most disaster-prone nations due to its [Pacific Ring of Fire](https://www.britannica.com/place/Ring-of-Fire) location, resulting in earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, and tsunamis. Its position in the western Pacific Ocean also exposes it to tropical cyclones, including typhoons. Just weeks before Typhoon Haiyan, a 7.2 magnitude earthquake struck the country’s [Bohol Island,](https://www.who.int/philippines/news/feature-stories/detail/bohol-earthquake-one-year-on) impacting 3 million people and claiming over 200 lives. As families were still recovering and housed in evacuation shelters, Typhoon Haiyan struck, compounding the challenges.

[BACK TO QUESTIONS](#FAQs)

![Men assess the damage of Typhoon Haiyan in 2013.](https://wvusstatic.com/email/met3/spacer.gif)

## How did World Vision respond to help people affected by Typhoon Haiyan?

Since 1954, World Vision has been actively working in the Philippines, dedicated to caring for children and fostering sustainable communities. Typhoon Haiyan devastated 20 program areas where we were actively involved, including in Bohol province, which had suffered significant damage from an earlier earthquake.

When Typhoon Haiyan hit, our local staff responded immediately. Even as they started their emergency relief distribution, a top priority was reaching out to nearly 40,000 children registered in World Vision’s sponsorship program. U.S. donors sponsored more than 5,600 children in Leyte and Antique provinces.

The Typhoon Haiyan response covered four areas — North Cebu, Panay, West Leyte, and East Leyte — encompassing 566 villages in 48 municipalities that bore the brunt of Haiyan’s impact. By August 2017, World Vision had reached over 1.6 million people during its three-year response, offering vital support to typhoon-impacted communities.

**Typhoon Haiyan, a Decade Later**
==================================

_Much has changed since the Super Typhoon Haiyan, known locally in the Philippines as Yolanda, devastated the Philippines 10 years ago. One of the most powerful storms in recorded history, its impacts are still present in the island nation, as well as in the minds of those who responded to it. Today, we remember what happened and how we worked hand-in-hand with affected communities to help them recover._

USAID deployed a 19-person Disaster Assistance Response Team (DART) to lead the U.S. humanitarian response to Typhoon Haiyan, one of the deadliest natural disasters in the history of the Philippines. Photos: USAID, AFP

“Echoing silence.”

Those are the words Ben Hemingway, USAID Regional Director for East Asia and the Pacific, used to describe the response he got when trying to make contact with humanitarian colleagues in Tacloban city in the immediate aftermath of Super Typhoon Haiyan making landfall in November 2013. He had arrived in Manila days earlier from Bangkok, Thailand, where he was based at the time.

That silence was in direct contrast to the super typhoon that had just crashed into the city with nearly 200 mile-per-hour winds and waves strong enough to slam massive ships on top of buildings.

Video courtesy: NOAA

It was another day-and-a-half before Hemingway and Nancy Ebuenga, [USAID Philippines](https://www.usaid.gov/philippines) Program Development Specialist, were able to make it to Tacloban. They were among the first international responders to reach the devastated city, and the scene that greeted them was unfathomable.

“No buildings. Infrastructure gone. Even roads seemed to have been erased by either debris from the storm surge or by the homes and buildings that collapsed around them. It was hard to even trace where roads would have been. There was just an absence,” Hemingway said.

![Image 5](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:3000/1*SYuCsI_XL5P9ZcE4Pegs8A.jpeg)

Super Typhoon Haiyan flattened homes and buildings in the city of Tacloban, Leyte province, in the central Philippines. Photo: AFP

**USAID Responds**
------------------

To this day, a decade later, Super Typhoon Haiyan remains one of the deadliest natural disasters in the history of the Philippines. More than 6,000 people were killed and 4.1 million displaced.

USAID’s response was swift. In addition to Hemingway and Ebuenga, [USAID’s Bureau for Humanitarian Assistance](https://www.usaid.gov/humanitarian-assistance) deployed a Disaster Assistance Response Team, commonly known as a DART, to lead the U.S. government’s robust response that included support from the U.S. Department of Defense and other U.S. government agencies.

VIDEO: USAID’s Disaster Assistance Response Team (DART) was one of the first international teams on the ground. DART members share what they saw right after Haiyan made landfall and what they did to save lives.

Over the course of the response, the United States provided approximately $143 million in humanitarian and development assistance, including providing critical food, health, protection, and shelter assistance, as well as water, sanitation, and hygiene support. The U.S. military supported USAID’s response by airlifting critical relief supplies to communities cut off by the storm. In addition, coordination between the U.S. military and the Philippine Air Force enabled Tacloban Airport to reopen after sustaining severe damage, allowing the rest of the world to help provide support.

![Image 6](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1000/1*w2HqAq5Qap-e2TICao-UHw.jpeg)

![Image 7](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4256/1*lZ3E1g3Y1MaBxiw_aMh8lQ.jpeg)

![Image 8](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:2144/1*vY_CcE1qGQGkoeWn6zwcjw.jpeg)

One way the U.S. military supported USAID’s response was by airlifting critical supplies to areas that had been cut off by the typhoon. Photos: USAID, AFP

The response, lessons learned from it, and 10 years of humanitarian and resilience programming have since left the Philippines more prepared and better equipped to respond to a disaster of a similar magnitude.

**Building Back Safer**
-----------------------

Shelter was one of the immediate priorities of USAID’s response, with more than a million homes damaged or destroyed during the storm. It has remained a cornerstone effort to prevent death and destruction during future disasters.

Chuck Setchell, USAID Senior Shelter and Settlements Advisor, recently arrived home from a trip to the Philippines. He’s been to the country many times. The first was as a Peace Corps volunteer in 1976. He returned as a member of the Typhoon Haiyan USAID DART, arriving at Tacloban’s recently reopened airport shortly after the storm hit.

![Image 9](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:700/1*ON591rNM7eK4GHZEYwqrew.jpeg)

Chuck Setchell, USAID Senior Shelter and Settlements Advisor, met with community leaders to hear about shelter needs. Photo: USAID

In the immediate aftermath of the storm transitional shelters projects were implemented.

“We needed to get a roof over people’s heads as soon as possible,” Setchell recalled, “but we also wanted to provide safe shelter, and help people make their communities safer than before.”

![Image 10](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1500/1*-CdPT2tyYBG-XK5DSr8N0Q.jpeg)

![Image 11](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4096/1*ovJbOBsAowqh9ajFHnOq5A.jpeg)

In the immediate aftermath of the typhoon, people were given plastic sheeting to construct emergency shelters. Other forms of transitional shelter, like the homes pictured above right, helped families like the Sampan family recover. Photos: USAID, CRS

On his recent trip he spoke with USAID partners [Catholic Relief Services (CRS)](https://www.crs.org/our-work-overseas/where-we-work/philippines) and [Plan Internationa](https://plan-international.org/philippines/)l and was able to conduct a site visit to reconstruction projects supported by other U.S. agencies. There are now more permanent shelters that families are now living in, including one- and two-story homes that were built to better withstand storms and in neighborhoods that are further from the low-lying coast and less susceptible to storm surge. Other measures, like solar lighting and improved and safer walkways, provide an additional layer of safety that wasn’t there before.

“These are safer shelters in safer settlements,” Setchell said.

**A New Way of Responding**
---------------------------

![Image 12](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:1000/1*FDyTMke1pG5hG9WGRw9TuA.jpeg)

This before and after picture shows the same road in Tacloban city on November 10, 2013 and November 5, 2018. Photos: AFP

Lessons learned from Haiyan have also led to a shift in the way Philippine authorities prepare for disasters. With help from USAID, the country has scaled up their investment in a robust response framework that includes prepositioning of supplies and early warning systems.

“One of our goals is to help build more response readiness capacity connecting the national level capabilities with the first responder needs of the affected communities,” Hemingway explained.

“For example, having logistics capabilities on standby to quickly secure trucks, planes, and boats to move commodities across the archipelago that is the Philippines is something that is core to the Philippine government’s response framework. Having more pre-positioning and warehouse capabilities across the country is something that was started by USAID through the U.N. and some of our NGO partners. Now most of that work is transitioned over to the government of Philippines owned warehouse facilities with their own commodities.”

![Image 13](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:3000/1*ltCXw4KtxU2zid5iGMhltQ.jpeg)

In December 2021, Super Typhoon Rai made landfall in the Philippines as a Category 5 storm. Photo: USAID

These efforts were put to the test after Super Typhoon Rai — known locally as Odette — struck in December 2021. The Philippine government response efforts, along with local partners, were literally able to help communities weather the storm themselves. And as a result of improved preparedness efforts, USAID did not need to deploy a DART following the typhoon despite the severity of the storm.

![Image 14](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:854/1*qnfbqfKrkGt9LzZqaaTMRA.jpeg)

![Image 15](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4032/1*YnGaKe9v8y6ATZgBAFrSoA.jpeg)

![Image 16](https://miro.medium.com/v2/resize:fit:4032/1*RsaI1RGBMIJNK2IZwIxd8w.jpeg)

USAID partners, like IOM, were able to use prepositioned USAID supplies to respond to Super Typhoon Rai. Photos: USAID

**To Be Strong**
----------------

The word resilient is often used when talking about the Philippines. Much of the work USAID has done to help people recover from Haiyan and prepare for future disasters is labeled “resilience” programming, and the Filipino people themselves are often said to be “resilient,” having persevered through volcanic eruptions, typhoons, earthquakes and more in recent years. In total, USAID has responded to more than 50 disasters in the Philippines since 1990.

But USAID Philippines’ Nancy Ebuenga, a proud Filipino, thinks another word is more appropriate.

“Filipinos are _matatag_,” she explained. “_Matatag_ means to be strong. Strong in the face of difficulty and in the face of losing everything and starting over. I think the reason Filipinos are matatag is because they know they can rely on their families and they can rely on their neighbors… It’s those tight-knit relationships you have that makes you strong.”

Ten years after Haiyan, USAID remembers the lives lost and upended by this catastrophe and reaffirms our commitment to work to harness that _matatag_ spirit of community in the Philippines in years to come.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Typhoon Haiyan, or Yolanda, struck the Philippines on November 8, 2013, as a Category 5 super typhoon, causing unprecedented damage across the region.
Haiyan resulted in over 6,300 fatalities, affected 14 million people, and caused damages exceeding $2.99 billion, devastating cities like Tacloban and Leyte.
Global humanitarian efforts included U.S. aid with $143 million in assistance, deploying teams for immediate relief, and coordinating with local agencies for effective response.
Filipino communities demonstrated resilience, leveraging support for recovery efforts, including rebuilding homes and infrastructure, and enhancing disaster preparedness for future events.
Post-Haiyan, strategies improved, focusing on better early warning systems, infrastructure planning, and community training to enhance resilience against future hydro-meteorological hazards.
